text: The surgeon clears tissue around the hernia defect to ensure no interference with the placement of the mesh, avoiding previously operated areas.
The video shows the previously placed mesh with good tissue integration with the surrounding structures, while a new hernia is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon excises the hernia sac to prevent post-operative seromas, followed by excision of peritoneum around the hernia defect to ensure unobstructed integration of a new mesh.
The robotic system is used to close the hernia defect, with precise suturing visible at the site.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 polypropylene barbed suture to close the surgical site, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon achieves a snug closure of the tissue using a two-zero polypropylene barbed suture in a continuous fashion.
The surgeon begins a second row of sutures using a two-zero polypropylene barbed suture, ensuring a continuous closure of the tissue defect.
The surgeon completes the adhesiolysis and places a mesh over the defect. The mesh is then fixed in place using trans fascial sutures at the twelve o'clock and six o'clock positions.
The surgeon begins the complete mesh fixation using three ports, including one for the camera and two for instruments, ensuring the mesh is properly oriented and initially secured.
Robotic instruments are used to flatten the mesh, preparing it for the completion of the suturing process.
The surgeon begins the first point of mesh fixation using a trans fascial suture, securing the mesh in place within the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to fixate the mesh at the next point, which is either the far end or at the three o'clock or nine o'clock position, depending on the mesh orientation.
The surgeon uses a polypropylene barbed suture to secure the mesh, ensuring careful placement due to a previous case of recurrence.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to suture the mesh in the right half of the patient's abdomen, demonstrating careful technique and dexterity required for iPOM plus procedures.
The surgeon uses Vicryl 3-0 sutures to take interrupted bites for fixation, ensuring a non-strangulating closure to avoid capturing nerves or large muscle chunks.
The surgeon demonstrates cutting the suture with free hand to reduce downtime, while holding the needle in place.
The surgeon prepares to begin suturing in the next step, focusing on the right half of the patient's surgical field.
The surgeon uses a needle driver in the right hand and a pro grasp in the left hand, demonstrating precise instrument choreography during the suturing process.
The surgeon displays a 15 by 15 centimeter Ventralite mesh, which is a dual-layered mesh, prepared for placement.
The surgeon is suturing in the right-sided superior quadrant, using a needle driver and pro grasp to achieve precise tissue apposition.
The surgeon begins suturing the left side of the mesh on the patient's left side, maintaining a distance of eight to ten centimeters from the mesh edge for optimal port placement.

The surgeon carefully sutures the mesh in the inferior quadrant, taking care to avoid the inferior epigastric vessels, which could cause bleeding or hematoma if inadvertently included in the suture bite.
The surgeon is nearing the completion of suturing the mesh, with visible progress in securing the mesh to the underlying tissue.
The surgeon observes the mesh, noting that it is positioned flatly within the surgical field.
